context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m working on Problem Set 6 for vibrations/waves, and i’m kinda stuck on 6.1 rn. can we start with the closed-open organ pipe modes?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's dig in! Before we draw anything, tell me: what physically happens to the air displacement at a closed end versus an open end of a pipe? What does each boundary condition look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting on 6.1, which involves understanding boundary conditions for a closed-open organ pipe. I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what the boundary conditions physically mean before jumping into any math. I'll ask a targeted opening question to assess their baseline understanding.

exchanges.student: i think closed end means the air can’t move, so displacement is 0 there? and open end is where displacement is biggest, like an antinode i think?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — node at x=0, antinode at x=L. Now, given those two constraints, what is the simplest (longest wavelength) wave shape that satisfies both at the same time? Try to think about what fraction of a full wavelength fits in the pipe.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both boundary conditions. I should affirm this and immediately push them to apply it to the problem rather than dwelling on the concept. The next step is to get them thinking about what kind of wave shape satisfies both conditions simultaneously.

exchanges.student: is it like a quarter wavelength fits in the pipe, so \(L=\lambda/4\)? so then \(\lambda=4L\)?
exchanges.tutor: Correct! That's your first normal mode. Now, can you figure out what the next allowed wavelength is? Remember, you still need a node at x=0 and an antinode at x=L — what's the next fraction of a wavelength that satisfies both conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the fundamental mode. I should confirm this and push them to extend the reasoning to the next two modes (n=2 and n=3) rather than dwelling on the fundamental. I want them to figure out the pattern themselves.

exchanges.student: umm maybe 3/4 of a wavelength? so \(L=3\lambda/4\) for the next one? i'm not totally sure why it skips 1/2 tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on 3/4! Now think about why 1/2 wavelength doesn't work: if you placed exactly half a wavelength in the pipe, what would you have at x=L — a node or an antinode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the second mode right but is questioning why half-wavelength is skipped. This is a good conceptual question worth addressing briefly with a Socratic nudge rather than explaining it directly. I'll ask them to think about what a half-wavelength shape would look like at both ends.

exchanges.student: half a wavelength would end at another node, right? so that fails bc the open end needs an antinode. so the allowed ones are odd quarters only.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — only odd multiples of λ/4. So you have your three wavelengths. Now, A in the wave equation gives you the wave speed. Can you figure out what that speed is, and then use it to calculate the frequencies for the first three modes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has fully grasped the pattern. I should confirm and immediately push them to apply this to get the three normal mode frequencies using the wave speed derived from A.

exchanges.student: i think \(v=\sqrt{A}=\sqrt{90000}=300\) m/s. then \(f=v/\lambda\), so with \(L=1.5\) m i get \(\lambda_1=6\), \(\lambda_2=2\), \(\lambda_3=1.2\) m maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Good, those wavelengths look right to me. Now go ahead and compute the three frequencies from f=v/λ — what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly found v=300 m/s and f=v/λ. The first wavelength λ1=4L=6m is correct. But λ2 and λ3 are wrong — they should be 4L/3=2m and 4L/5=1.2m respectively. Wait, let me recheck: λ1=4L=6m ✓, λ2=4L/3=2m ✓, λ3=4L/5=1.2m ✓. Actually those are all correct! Now I should push them to compute the actual frequencies.

exchanges.student: ok then \(f_1=300/6=50\) Hz, \(f_2=300/2=150\) Hz, and \(f_3=300/1.2=250\) Hz. so it’s the odd harmonics, nice.
exchanges.tutor: Correct — 50, 150, 250 Hz, odd multiples of the fundamental. Now for part (a) you also need the actual sketches of ψ vs x. Given what we said about nodes and antinodes, can you describe what each of the three mode shapes looks like over 0 ≤ x ≤ L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed all three frequencies and recognized the odd harmonic pattern. I should confirm and move them forward to part (a)'s drawing requirement, and then parts (b) and (c), rather than dwelling here.

exchanges.student: i think mode 1 is one quarter of a sine, mode 2 is three quarters, and mode 3 is five quarters, all starting at 0 at \(x=0\) and ending at max displacement at \(x=L\). can u check if that’s the right way to say the sketches?
exchanges.tutor: That description is correct. Now for part (b), the pressure variation is related to but not the same as the displacement. Think about this: where displacement is maximum (antinode), what is happening to the air compression — is the pressure variation large or small there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly described the three mode shapes. I should confirm briefly and move them on to part (b), which asks about pressure rather than displacement. This is a conceptually distinct step — pressure nodes and antinodes are swapped relative to displacement.

exchanges.student: i always mix this up ngl — i think at displacement antinode the pressure variation is 0, so pressure has a node there?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly — pressure and displacement are 90° out of phase spatially: displacement antinodes are pressure nodes and vice versa. So for part (b), how does that swap change the sketches at x=0 and x=L compared to what you drew for part (a)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the key relationship but seems uncertain. I should affirm and push them to apply this swap to sketch all three pressure mode shapes, rather than dwelling on the concept.

exchanges.student: then pressure should have an antinode at the closed end and a node at the open end, so it kinda swaps the ends from the displacement sketch. i think the first 3 pressure shapes are the same odd-quarter pattern, just shifted so max at \(x=0\) and zero at \(x=L\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now let's move to part (c). If the pipe is open at both ends, what are the boundary conditions, and what is the simplest wavelength that fits? Then you can figure out what length preserves the 50 Hz fundamental.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly described the pressure mode shapes. I should confirm and move them to part (c), which is a distinct and straightforward calculation about changing the pipe to open-open and matching the fundamental frequency.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently prompting the student to derive each step rather than providing answers directly. Scaffolding was well-structured, building from boundary conditions to wavelengths to frequencies to sketches to pressure. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, and communication was concise and appropriately supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave away answers; each turn posed a targeted question that led the student to the next step. The student generated all key ideas (node/antinode conditions, odd-quarter wavelengths, speed calculation, frequencies, pressure-displacement phase relationship). No near-submission-ready output was produced. 1.2: The tutor built directly on each correct student response and did not restart or ignore valid progress; no diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor provided implicit process feedback by affirming correct reasoning steps and redirecting with targeted questions, which is sufficient for this physics problem-solving context. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-paced, and maintained a warm, encouraging tone without implying formal grading.
